main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Automated data construction method for conversational search dataset creation considering multi-intent and contextual dependency","Use of a QA tool to extract evidence and generate multiple intent-oriented natural language queries from keyword-based queries","Construction of query trees to represent unique de-contextualized search conversations","Reverse query reformulation to convert sessions into context-dependent conversations","Development of a dialogue discriminator trained with contrastive learning to filter defective conversations and ensure consistency and coherence","Meticulous data cleaning and quality control pipeline"],"problems":["Lack of large-scale, high-quality conversational search datasets","Existing datasets overlook multi-intent and contextual information","Bias in datasets where all dialogue queries link to a single positive passage","Difficulty in gathering real-world conversational search data","Quality issues in synthetic conversational search datasets"],"datasets":["ICConv (Intent-oriented and Context-aware Conversational search dataset)","MS MARCO (used as source for session data)"],"metrics":["Human evaluation based on six dialogue and search related criteria","Statistical analysis of dataset properties"],"results":[{"metric":"Number of conversations in ICConv","value":"over 100,000"},{"metric":"Human evaluation (six criteria)","value":"ICConv performed admirably (exact scores not specified)"}],"novelty_claims":["First large-scale synthetic conversational search dataset that incorporates both multi-intent and context-aware features","Novel automated data construction method that generates multiple natural language queries per keyword query based on latent intents and contextualizes them within sessions","Use of a dialogue discriminator trained with contrastive learning to ensure conversational consistency and coherence","ICConv relieves the data scarcity problem in conversational search and provides a new standard for evaluation"]}

methods: Automated data construction method for conversational search dataset creation considering multi-intent and contextual dependency
Use of a QA tool to extract evidence and generate multiple intent-oriented natural language queries from keyword-based queries
Construction of query trees to represent unique de-contextualized search conversations
Reverse query reformulation to convert sessions into context-dependent conversations
Development of a dialogue discriminator trained with contrastive learning to filter defective conversations and ensure consistency and coherence
Meticulous data cleaning and quality control pipeline
problems: Lack of large-scale, high-quality conversational search datasets
Existing datasets overlook multi-intent and contextual information
Bias in datasets where all dialogue queries link to a single positive passage
Difficulty in gathering real-world conversational search data
Quality issues in synthetic conversational search datasets
datasets: ICConv (Intent-oriented and Context-aware Conversational search dataset)
MS MARCO (used as source for session data)
metrics: Human evaluation based on six dialogue and search related criteria

results.value: ICConv performed admirably (exact scores not specified)

novelty_claims: First large-scale synthetic conversational search dataset that incorporates both multi-intent and context-aware features
Novel automated data construction method that generates multiple natural language queries per keyword query based on latent intents and contextualizes them within sessions
Use of a dialogue discriminator trained with contrastive learning to ensure conversational consistency and coherence
ICConv relieves the data scarcity problem in conversational search and provides a new standard for evaluation

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["ConvTrans: a data augmentation method that transforms web search sessions into conversational search sessions","Graph-based reorganization of web search sessions into heterogeneous session graphs with response-induced, topic-shared, and topic-changed edges","Conversational query rewriting using two T5 models to convert keyword-based queries into conversational natural language queries","Random walk sampling on the session graph to generate pseudo conversational search sessions"],"problems":["Data scarcity for training conversational dense retrieval models due to lack of large-scale real conversational search sessions and relevance labels","Gap between web search sessions and conversational search sessions in terms of session quality and query form, making direct use of web sessions ineffective for conversational dense retrieval"],"datasets":["MS MARCO search sessions (used as source for data augmentation)","CAsT-19 (Conversational Assistance Track 2019)","CAsT-20 (Conversational Assistance Track 2020)"],"metrics":["Retrieval performance (e.g., NDCG, MRR, Recall; specific metrics not explicitly named in the provided text but standard for retrieval tasks)"],"results":[{"metric":"Retrieval performance (on CAsT-19 and CAsT-20)","value":"Comparable to models trained on high-quality but expensive artificial conversational search data"}],"novelty_claims":["ConvTrans is the first method to automatically transform web search sessions into pseudo conversational search sessions for data augmentation in conversational dense retrieval","ConvTrans uses a novel graph-based reorganization and conversational query rewriting approach to bridge the gap between web and conversational search sessions","ConvTrans enables large-scale, low-cost generation of conversational search data, reducing reliance on manual data generation and addressing the cold start problem for conversational search systems"]}

methods: ConvTrans: a data augmentation method that transforms web search sessions into conversational search sessions
Graph-based reorganization of web search sessions into heterogeneous session graphs with response-induced, topic-shared, and topic-changed edges
Conversational query rewriting using two T5 models to convert keyword-based queries into conversational natural language queries
Random walk sampling on the session graph to generate pseudo conversational search sessions
problems: Data scarcity for training conversational dense retrieval models due to lack of large-scale real conversational search sessions and relevance labels
Gap between web search sessions and conversational search sessions in terms of session quality and query form, making direct use of web sessions ineffective for conversational dense retrieval
datasets: MS MARCO search sessions (used as source for data augmentation)
metrics: Retrieval performance (e.g., NDCG, MRR, Recall; specific metrics not explicitly named in the provided text but standard for retrieval tasks)

results.value: Comparable to models trained on high-quality but expensive artificial conversational search data

novelty_claims: ConvTrans is the first method to automatically transform web search sessions into pseudo conversational search sessions for data augmentation in conversational dense retrieval
ConvTrans uses a novel graph-based reorganization and conversational query rewriting approach to bridge the gap between web and conversational search sessions
ConvTrans enables large-scale, low-cost generation of conversational search data, reducing reliance on manual data generation and addressing the cold start problem for conversational search systems

methods: LAPS (LLM-Augmented Personalized Self-Dialogue): uses LLMs to guide a single human worker in generating personalized, multi-session dialogues, extracting and storing user preferences in a preference memory

results.value: LAPS outperforms LLM-generated dialogues and matches or exceeds expert-created datasets

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Prompting large language models (LLMs) as query rewriters using well-designed instructions incorporating four essential properties of well-formed rewrites","Prompting LLMs as rewrite editors in a 'rewrite-then-edit' process, where LLMs edit initial rewrites to improve informativeness","Distilling the rewriting capabilities of LLMs into smaller models to reduce rewriting latency and cost"],"problems":["Human-rewritten queries for conversational search often lack sufficient informativeness for optimal retrieval performance","Existing query rewriting models trained on human rewrites achieve sub-optimal performance due to missing contextual information","High cost, workload, and inconsistency issues with using human annotators for more informative rewrites"],"datasets":["QReCC"],"metrics":["Retrieval performance (likely measured by standard IR metrics such as NDCG, Recall, or MRR, though not explicitly stated in the excerpt)"],"results":[{"metric":"Retrieval performance (sparse retriever)","value":"Informative query rewrites from LLMs substantially outperform human rewrites"},{"metric":"Retrieval performance (dense retriever)","value":"Informative query rewrites from LLMs outperform human rewrites, but the improvement is especially notable for sparse retrievers"}],"novelty_claims":["First to introduce the concept of informative conversational query rewriting and identify four desirable properties for well-crafted rewrites","Propose prompting LLMs as both query rewriters and rewrite editors with instructions incorporating all desirable properties","Employ distillation to transfer LLM rewriting capabilities into smaller, more efficient models","Demonstrate that informative query rewrites generated by LLMs can yield substantially improved retrieval performance compared to human rewrites, especially for sparse retrievers"]}

methods: Prompting large language models (LLMs) as query rewriters using well-designed instructions incorporating four essential properties of well-formed rewrites
Prompting LLMs as rewrite editors in a 'rewrite-then-edit' process, where LLMs edit initial rewrites to improve informativeness
Distilling the rewriting capabilities of LLMs into smaller models to reduce rewriting latency and cost
problems: Human-rewritten queries for conversational search often lack sufficient informativeness for optimal retrieval performance

metrics: Retrieval performance (likely measured by standard IR metrics such as NDCG, Recall, or MRR, though not explicitly stated in the excerpt)

results.value: Informative query rewrites from LLMs substantially outperform human rewrites

novelty_claims: First to introduce the concept of informative conversational query rewriting and identify four desirable properties for well-crafted rewrites

Employ distillation to transfer LLM rewriting capabilities into smaller, more efficient models
Demonstrate that informative query rewrites generated by LLMs can yield substantially improved retrieval performance compared to human rewrites, especially for sparse retrievers

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["ConvGQR framework: combines query rewriting and query expansion for conversational search","Query rewriting using a generative pre-trained language model (PLM) to mimic human-rewritten queries","Query expansion by generating potential answers using a generative PLM and incorporating them into the query","Knowledge infusion mechanism: optimizes query reformulation with guidance from passage retrieval, integrating retrieval signals into the reformulation process"],"problems":["Difficulty in determining the user's real search intent in conversational search due to dependency on conversation history","Existing query rewriting models are sub-optimal because they mimic human-rewritten queries, which are not always the best for retrieval","Separation of query rewriting and query expansion in prior work, missing their complementary effects","Lack of direct optimization of query reformulation for retrieval performance"],"datasets":["Four conversational search datasets (specific names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Retrieval performance metrics (e.g., passage ranking metrics such as NDCG, MRR, or Recall; specific metrics not provided in the excerpt)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes ConvGQR, a new framework that integrates both query rewriting and query expansion for conversational search, leveraging generative PLMs for both tasks","Introduces a novel use of generated potential answers for query expansion, exploiting PLMs' world knowledge","Designs a knowledge infusion mechanism that directly optimizes query reformulation with retrieval guidance, linking reformulation to downstream retrieval performance","Demonstrates the complementary effects of query rewriting and query expansion in improving conversational search effectiveness"]}

methods: Formulation of the novel task of Multimodal Query Clarification (MQC) in conversational search
Collection and creation of the Melon dataset (multimodal clarifying questions with images)
Proposal of Marto, a multimodal query clarification model based on a generative, prompt-based fine-tuning strategy
Multi-task fine-tuning of Marto for both classification (is a question multimodal?) and retrieval (selecting relevant images and documents)
Comparative evaluation with discriminative baselines (e.g., VisualBERT) and unimodal models
Analysis of the effect of attaching different images (relevant vs. random) to clarifying questions

Ambiguity and incompleteness in user queries leading to poor retrieval results
Lack of multimodal (image+text) clarifying questions in existing conversational search systems

results.value: up to 90% improvement
0.77 vs. 8.26 (Marto is faster)
0.67 vs. 1.13 (Marto is faster)

Demonstration that adding relevant images to clarifying questions significantly improves retrieval performance and user response quality

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["RETPO (Retriever's Preference Optimization): a framework that aligns a language model for query rewriting with retriever's preferences by generating multiple rewrites, collecting retrieval feedback, and fine-tuning a smaller LM to prefer rewrites that perform better in retrieval.","Construction of RF COLLECTION: a large-scale dataset of retriever feedback on over 410K query rewrites across 12K conversations.","Fine-tuning Llama2-7b using preference-driven optimization based on retriever feedback."],"problems":["Sub-optimal query rewrites in conversational search due to lack of retriever feedback in training.","Difficulty in aligning question rewriting models with the preferences of retrieval systems for improved search performance.","Existing human rewrites for query reformulation do not consider retrieval effectiveness, leading to less effective search queries."],"datasets":["QReCC (Question Rewriting in Conversational Context)","TopiOCQA","RF COLLECTION (Retriever's Feedback Collection, constructed in this work)"],"metrics":["MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank)","Recall@10","GPT-4 evaluation of rewrite quality (clarity and informativeness)"],"results":[{"metric":"MRR (QReCC)","value":"11.8 points higher than BM25"},{"metric":"Recall@10 (QReCC)","value":"19.0 points higher than BM25"},{"metric":"GPT-4 evaluation (rewrite quality)","value":"RETPO rewrites favored over human rewrites in clarity and informativeness"}],"novelty_claims":["RETPO is the first framework to leverage retriever preference-driven optimization for query reformulation in conversational search.","Construction and release of RF COLLECTION, a large-scale dataset of retriever feedback on query rewrites in dialogue.","Alignment of an open-source LM (Llama2-7b) with retriever preferences, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on QReCC and TopiOCQA benchmarks.","RETPO rewrites are more specific, detailed, and effective than human rewrites and previous baselines, including GPT-3.5 and GPT-4."]}

methods: RETPO (Retriever's Preference Optimization): a framework that aligns a language model for query rewriting with retriever's preferences by generating multiple rewrites, collecting retrieval feedback, and fine-tuning a smaller LM to prefer rewrites that perform better in retrieval.
Construction of RF COLLECTION: a large-scale dataset of retriever feedback on over 410K query rewrites across 12K conversations.
Fine-tuning Llama2-7b using preference-driven optimization based on retriever feedback.
problems: Sub-optimal query rewrites in conversational search due to lack of retriever feedback in training.
Difficulty in aligning question rewriting models with the preferences of retrieval systems for improved search performance.
Existing human rewrites for query reformulation do not consider retrieval effectiveness, leading to less effective search queries.
datasets: RF COLLECTION (Retriever's Feedback Collection, constructed in this work)
metrics: GPT-4 evaluation of rewrite quality (clarity and informativeness)

results.value: 11.8 points higher than BM25
19.0 points higher than BM25
RETPO rewrites favored over human rewrites in clarity and informativeness

novelty_claims: RETPO is the first framework to leverage retriever preference-driven optimization for query reformulation in conversational search.
Construction and release of RF COLLECTION, a large-scale dataset of retriever feedback on query rewrites in dialogue.
Alignment of an open-source LM (Llama2-7b) with retriever preferences, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on QReCC and TopiOCQA benchmarks.
RETPO rewrites are more specific, detailed, and effective than human rewrites and previous baselines, including GPT-3.5 and GPT-4.

methods: AdaCQR framework: aligns reformulation models with both term-based (sparse) and semantic-based (dense) retrieval systems via a two-stage training strategy
Fusion metric: evaluates generalization performance of reformulated queries across retrieval systems
Uncertainty estimation and contrastive learning: guides LLMs to generate superior reformulation labels
Diverse Beam Search: gathers multiple candidate reformulation queries
Contrastive loss: aligns reformulation model from both term and semantic perspectives using candidate queries and their relative orders
problems: Generalization of conversational query reformulation (CQR) across both sparse (term-based) and dense (semantic-based) retrieval systems
Instability and complexity in previous alignment methods (e.g., MBR, reinforcement learning)
Reliance on sub-optimal and costly human-annotated reformulation labels
Lack of alignment between training objectives and retrieval task targets in CQR
metrics: Retrieval performance metrics (not specified, but likely include standard IR metrics such as NDCG, Recall, MRR, etc.)
Fusion metric (proposed in the paper to evaluate generalization across retrieval systems)
novelty_claims: AdaCQR is the first framework to align reformulation models with both term-based and semantic-based retrieval systems, enhancing generalizability across diverse retrieval environments.
Introduction of a fusion metric to evaluate and guide the generalization of reformulated queries across retrieval systems.
A two-stage training strategy that uses uncertainty estimation and contrastive learning to generate superior labels and diverse candidate queries without relying on human annotation.
Reference-free evaluation and alignment using contrastive loss from both term and semantic perspectives.
Demonstrated significant improvements over existing methods on multiple benchmark conversational search datasets.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Construction of MIntRec2.0, a large-scale multimodal multi-party conversational intent dataset with detailed annotations for intent and speaker identity, including out-of-scope (OOS) detection.","A general framework for multimodal intent recognition and out-of-scope detection in both single-turn and multi-turn conversations, including: (a) organization of data at utterance and dialogue levels, (b) extraction of text, video, and audio features, (c) multimodal fusion using strong fusion methods (e.g., Tsai et al., 2019; Rahman et al., 2020), (d) training with multimodal fusion loss and cross-entropy loss for in-scope and out-of-scope data, (e) threshold-based inference for OOS detection.","Benchmarking with classic multimodal fusion methods, ChatGPT, and human evaluators."],"problems":["Lack of large-scale, high-quality multimodal intent recognition datasets, especially for multi-party, multi-turn conversations.","Existing datasets do not adequately address out-of-scope (OOS) detection in real-world conversational scenarios.","Difficulty in leveraging nonverbal (video, audio) and contextual information for intent recognition and OOS detection.","Performance gap between machine learning models (including LLMs) and humans in multimodal intent recognition tasks."],"datasets":["MIntRec2.0 (introduced in this paper)","ATIS","Snips","CLINC150","MDID","Intentonomy","MIntRec (previous version)"],"metrics":["Accuracy (for intent recognition)","OOS detection robustness (likely measured by F1 or similar, though not explicitly stated)"],"results":[{"metric":"Human accuracy (few-shot, 7% training data)","value":"71%"},{"metric":"Performance gap (LLMs vs. humans)","value":"over 30% absolute accuracy difference"}],"novelty_claims":["MIntRec2.0 is the first large-scale multimodal multi-party conversational intent dataset with detailed annotations for both intent and speaker identity, supporting out-of-scope detection in open-world scenarios.","The dataset includes 1,245 dialogues, 15,040 samples, 30 fine-grained intent classes, and both in-scope and out-of-scope samples, with multimodal (text, video, audio) information.","Provides a universal framework for in-scope classification and out-of-scope detection in both single-turn and multi-turn conversations, with strong benchmark baselines.","Demonstrates the significant challenge of multimodal intent recognition, as evidenced by the large performance gap between LLMs and humans, highlighting the value of the dataset for advancing research."]}

methods: Construction of MIntRec2.0, a large-scale multimodal multi-party conversational intent dataset with detailed annotations for intent and speaker identity, including out-of-scope (OOS) detection.

A general framework for multimodal intent recognition and out-of-scope detection in both single-turn and multi-turn conversations, including: (a) organization of data at utterance and dialogue levels, (b) extraction of text, video, and audio features, (c) multimodal fusion using strong fusion methods (e.g., Tsai et al., 2019; Rahman et al., 2020), (d) training with multimodal fusion loss and cross-entropy loss for in-scope and out-of-scope data, (e) threshold-based inference for OOS detection.
Benchmarking with classic multimodal fusion methods, ChatGPT, and human evaluators.
problems: Lack of large-scale, high-quality multimodal intent recognition datasets, especially for multi-party, multi-turn conversations.
Existing datasets do not adequately address out-of-scope (OOS) detection in real-world conversational scenarios.
Difficulty in leveraging nonverbal (video, audio) and contextual information for intent recognition and OOS detection.
Performance gap between machine learning models (including LLMs) and humans in multimodal intent recognition tasks.
metrics: OOS detection robustness (likely measured by F1 or similar, though not explicitly stated)

results.value: over 30% absolute accuracy difference

novelty_claims: MIntRec2.0 is the first large-scale multimodal multi-party conversational intent dataset with detailed annotations for both intent and speaker identity, supporting out-of-scope detection in open-world scenarios.
The dataset includes 1,245 dialogues, 15,040 samples, 30 fine-grained intent classes, and both in-scope and out-of-scope samples, with multimodal (text, video, audio) information.
Provides a universal framework for in-scope classification and out-of-scope detection in both single-turn and multi-turn conversations, with strong benchmark baselines.

Demonstrates the significant challenge of multimodal intent recognition, as evidenced by the large performance gap between LLMs and humans, highlighting the value of the dataset for advancing research.
